macro_segments.title: Shechita She'eina Re'uya and Chullin Ba'azarah: Biblical vs. Rabbinic Status

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Shechita She'eina Re'uya and its application to Chullin Ba'azarah
The Gemara's implicit distinction between biblical and rabbinic law regarding formal technicalities
Does Rabbi Shimon hold Chullin Ba'azarah is biblical, and the proof from the Mishna about burning
Why extension to animals that cannot be Korbanot (deer) proves the law is biblical

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Chiya and the Blood of a Bird: Shechita Requirements and Alternatives

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Chiya's infestation problem and Rabbi's solution: making the bird a Treifa before Shechita
Rav Dimi's version: make the bird a Treifa; Ravin's version: kill it without Shechita
Why Rav Dimi could not simply say to kill without Shechita: the debate over whether Shechita is biblical for birds
The Mishna's ruling (Rabbi's version) that Shechita is biblical for birds based on 'Kaasher Tziviticha'
Why wanting the blood does not exempt from covering it, and the two ways to avoid covering blood

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Chiya's Merit and the Absence of Infestation: Righteous People and Personal Suffering

micro_segments.title: How could Rabbi Chiya have had an infestation when righteous people are supposed to be protected from such things
The statement of the heavenly voice about Chananya sustaining himself on minimal food despite supporting the world
The philosophical reflection on righteous people sacrificing for others while neglecting themselves

macro_segments.title: Cheresh, Shoteh, and Katan: Shechita by Unfit Individuals and the Role of Observation

micro_segments.title: The Mishna: if observed, their Shechita is valid; if not observed, concern about validity
Covering blood: if not observed, exempt from covering (lest we send the message it was valid)
Shechting animal and child: if not observed, Rabbi Meir permits shechting the child same day
The difference between covering blood (active action, sends message) and not shechting (passive, no message sent)
Concern about violating a prohibition overrides concern about messages; no lashes if uncertain

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Question: Why Does the Mishna Distinguish Between Covering Blood and Shechting the Child?

micro_segments.title: The Gemara asks why the beginning case forbids covering blood (if not observed) but the end case forbids shechting
The resolution: the Rabbis ignore message concerns and treat both as regular Safek cases
Rabbi Meir's position: treats unobserved Shechita as definitely invalid, going by Rov

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Meir and Minority Positions: The Connection Between Tzarich L'Miyuta and Status Quo

micro_segments.title: The challenge: if Rabbi Meir is concerned for minorities, why assume the majority did it badly?
The Gemara's resolution: Rov cannot change Chazaka; minority attaches to status quo
The precedent from the Mishna about an infant found by dough (Rov Tinokot Metapchin)
Why the dough case (ruling, not new action) differs from shechting case (permitting new action)
Tosafot's distinction: Tumah Ve'Tahara has its own leniencies; other laws do not

macro_segments.title: Rabbi's Rulings and the Search for His Final Position on Cheresh Shoteh Katan

micro_segments.title: Rabbi ruled both like Rabbi Meir and like the Chachamim in different places
The question in the Beis Medrash: which was Rabbi's last and authoritative ruling
Rabbi Zeira regrets not staying in the Beis Medrash to ask Rabbi Abba, who had the answer from his father
The proof: Rabbi Elazar sent word that Rabbi ruled like Rabbi Meir (definitely not valid)

macro_segments.title: Covering Blood of Multiple Animals and the Distinction Between Chaya and Of

micro_segments.title: One covering for many Chayot or many birds shechted in one place
Chaya and Of together can also be covered together
Rabbi Yehuda disagrees: separate coverings for Chaya and Of based on Torah's distinction
